topic.what: Analysis of how Traditional Persian Medicine (TPM, a form of Unani/Greco-Islamic medicine) conceptualises and manages functional dyspepsia (FD) through the framework of temperament theory (mizaj), with Sue-e-mizaj (intemperament or dyscrasia of the stomach) as the primary diagnostic category.
topic.how: Survey and synthesis of classical TPM/Unani texts (including Ibn Sina, Jorjani, and other Persian medical authorities) combined with review of modern functional dyspepsia literature; maps TPM diagnostic categories (hot/cold/moist/dry gastric intemperament subtypes) to Rome III/IV criteria and modern pathophysiological mechanisms; identifies traditional herbal and non-herbal treatments per temperament subtype.
topic.why_matters: Functional dyspepsia affects up to 20% of the global population with inadequate biomedical treatment options; TPM's temperament-based subtyping offers a potential patient stratification framework that could identify responder subgroups, and its herbal repertoire by temperament subtype constitutes an untapped pharmacological resource for FD management.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number and granularity of diagnostic subtypes for FD-equivalent conditions in TPM
characteristics.value: Four primary intemperament subtypes (hot, cold, moist, dry) each with further compound forms; maps to postprandial distress and epigastric pain syndromes differentially
characteristics.vs_baseline: Rome IV two-subtype FD classification (PDS, EPS)
characteristics.evidence: Classical textual analysis synthesised in the review
characteristics.context: Richness of classification does not imply clinical validity without prospective testing

characteristics.what_it_measures: Availability of specific herbal and dietary recommendations matched to temperament-based FD subtype
characteristics.value: Distinct herbal recommendations for each of the four intemperament subtypes; e.g., warming herbs for cold-type, cooling herbs for hot-type Sue-e-mizaj
characteristics.vs_baseline: Single-treatment-fits-all approach in current FD management
characteristics.evidence: Synthesised from classical TPM materia medica texts
characteristics.context: Treatment recommendations are textual; pharmacological validation per subtype largely absent

characteristics.what_it_measures: How well TPM Sue-e-mizaj subtypes correspond to known FD pathophysiology (motility, acid sensitivity, visceral hypersensitivity)
characteristics.value: Partial correspondence: hot intemperament maps loosely to hypersecretory/inflammatory states; cold intemperament to motility-delay; moist to mucus excess; dry to atrophic patterns
characteristics.evidence: Authors' interpretive mapping in discussion; not empirically validated
characteristics.context: Speculative cross-paradigm mapping requiring prospective validation

good_for: Generating a temperament-based patient stratification hypothesis for functional dyspepsia clinical trials
Identifying an untapped herbal repertoire for FD differentiated by temperament subtype
Understanding Persian/Iranian traditional medicine as a Unani-adjacent system with overlapping but distinct diagnostic categories
Informing culturally sensitive gastroenterology in Iranian and broader MENA/South Asian patient populations
Cross-paradigm concept extraction: mizaj temperature axis as a candidate biomarker-stratification hypothesis (e.g., inflammatory vs. non-inflammatory FD)
not_for: Clinical management decisions — diagnostic ontology not validated against biomedical endpoints
Drug selection without phytochemical and pharmacological validation of temperament-specific herbs
Replacing Rome IV criteria in clinical or research settings
requires: Prospective study correlating mizaj assessment with measurable biomedical FD subtypes (gastric emptying, acid sensitivity, mucosal inflammation)
Randomised trials of temperament-matched vs. unmatched herbal treatment in FD
Validated mizaj assessment instruments with inter-rater reliability data

novelty: Provides the most detailed analysis of Sue-e-mizaj as a diagnostic category specifically for functional dyspepsia, mapping it systematically against modern Rome IV criteria

Introduces the mizaj-stratified therapeutic approach as a precision medicine hypothesis — the idea that temperament subtype predicts herbal treatment response is a novel cross-paradigm hypothesis generator

Positions TPM not as a uniform 'traditional' block but as a subtype-differentiating diagnostic system with potential patient stratification value, a conceptual advance over generic integrative medicine claims
open_problems: No prospective validation that mizaj assessment predicts measurable FD pathophysiology (motility, acid, mucosal)
Randomised trial of temperament-matched herbal therapy for FD not yet conducted
Inter-rater reliability of mizaj diagnosis not assessed in modern clinical settings
Biochemical basis of the four-quality (hot/cold/moist/dry) framework not linked to specific biomarkers
Overlap and boundaries between Amlapitta (Ayurvedic) and hot Sue-e-mizaj (TPM) not systematically compared — cross-traditional disease ontology mapping absent
